To: M. Reyes
From: PC
Subject: CR-2024-004 audit — before you send to L.

M — careful read of the draft. The CR as written cannot responsibly go to L. today; it would either get approved on a partial picture (silent scope absorption with PM cover) or sent back in front of L., which is worse for you than us catching it now. Below by section.

1. IMPACT ANALYSIS — ONE VARIABLE COVERED, FOUR MISSING

The draft covers effort only (8 person-days). A CR's impact analysis covers all five variables: scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk.

- Scope: tags touch the customer table schema (data migration), CRM integration (R. Singh's team, external), self-service UI (UX), and accessibility (WCAG-AA). Name all four, not "customer-tags feature on the portal."
- Schedule: self-service is on Q3 critical path. What slips in exchange? When does the inserted work happen?
- Cost: 8 days is dev-only. Are we including UX, QA, accessibility, the CRM team's effort under their separate SOW? My back-of-envelope: 25-40 person-days across teams.
- Quality: WCAG-AA review on the tag interface; internationalisation; tag-naming conflicts; per-account limits.
- Risk: the CRM team's separate change-board (4-6 week lead time) is a dependency we don't currently carry; the V. Bauer prior-scope decision creates a governance risk if unacknowledged.

Ask M. for: cross-team estimate from UX/QA/data/CRM; named displacement in the sprint plan; CRM-side schema timeline; accessibility consultation status.

2. ROUTING TIER — THIS IS NOT TIER 1

Documented thresholds: Tier 1 = under 5 person-days AND no external deps AND no critical-path impact. Tier 2 = under 15 days OR modest external deps. Tier 3 = Steering, anything beyond.

CR-2024-004 fails Tier 1 on all three criteria: 8 days exceeds the 5-day cap on its own; the CRM team is an external dep on a separate change board; self-service is on critical path. The 8-day estimate puts it at Tier 2 minimum. The realistic 25-40-day cross-team + CRM external + critical-path makes it Tier 3 (Steering). If this goes Tier 1 and L. approves it, we've set a precedent that the documented thresholds are negotiable — which makes the next CR harder to route correctly.

3. POLITICS — THREE THINGS THIS CR HIDES

The CR presents the change as cost-neutral. It is not. Three trade-offs need to be surfaced before approval, not after.

(a) Who pays for the effort. The self-service team is at full capacity. 8 days (or 25-40) comes from deferral, overtime (we have a programme commitment against this), or unbudgeted contractors. The CR is stronger as "we propose to defer stories X and Y by N days to absorb this" than as "no impact."

(b) V. Bauer's prior scope decision. Six months ago V. Bauer (then CDO) deliberately excluded customer-tags because of CRM downstream complications. Re-introducing without acknowledging that history reads as ignorance or selective memory. Better: "The 2024-06 scope decision excluded tags pending CRM resolution. Since then [what changed]; we propose revisiting because [why]."

(c) Stakeholder asymmetry. J. Patel is 3 weeks in. A. Wong has been on the project from the start and would know the V. Bauer history but hasn't raised it. Ask A. Wong directly before finalising: why this, why now, and is "nice-to-have" the right framing if V. Bauer's reasoning still holds.

Hidden politics erupt in the approval meeting; surfaced politics get worked through before it.

4. ALTERNATIVES AND RECOMMENDATION — BOTH MISSING

The CR proposes a single path. Future readers want to see alternatives evaluated. M. should document and recommend among:

(a) Defer to phase 2 (post-Q3). Lowest risk; honours the V. Bauer decision; addresses A. Wong via a planned phase-2 commitment.
(b) Reduced-scope: system-defined tags only (fixed set of 8-10), no custom tags. Avoids the CRM schema change. ~5 person-days self-service only.
(c) MVP for Q3: self-service display only, no CRM write-through; full integration deferred.
(d) Build as scoped with honest costs and Tier 3 routing.

M. should recommend one with reasoning and let L. (or Steering) make the call. A CR without alternatives forces the approver to do the analyst's work in the meeting.

5. CHANGE LOG — THE BROADER PATTERN

Three entries in four months on a nine-month project of this scope is not "few changes happened" — it is silent absorption. Typical projects see ten to twenty percent scope growth through changes; we'd expect eight to twelve entries by now if changes were visible.

M. submitting this through the process is the behaviour we want to reinforce — worth thanking him while sending the audit back. The broader silent-absorption pattern I need to raise with L. separately, not in this CR. My read: the process feels heavy and people work around it; the fix is visible follow-through (three entries in four months means people don't see the log working). Worth a 15-min 1:1 with L. about asking each workstream lead to back-fill any changes informally absorbed.

CLOSING

Take the CR back today. Get the cross-team estimate. Talk to A. Wong about the V. Bauer history. Consult R. Singh on CRM-side feasibility. Add the four alternatives with your recommendation. Resubmit at Tier 2 or Tier 3 depending on final magnitude. Half a day to a day of your time, and the approval meeting takes 20 minutes instead of 90.

PC
